summary: Drew DeVault revisits his 2022 skepticism about Rust in the Linux kernel, this time with more compassion for the burned-out developers involved. He acknowledges the talent and dedication of Rust-for-Linux contributors while noting that Linux's decentralized maintainer culture has made the project a 'burnout machine.' Rather than continuing to fight LKML politics, he proposes an alternative path: building a Linux-compatible kernel from scratch in Rust, arguing this could move faster and attract more contributors without the political overhead.
key_points: Rust-for-Linux has become a burnout machine due to Linux's decentralized maintainer culture where every subsystem is a 'private fiefdom'
Introducing Rust to Linux is 'one part coding work and ninety-nine parts political work'
A Linux-compatible kernel written from scratch in Rust could reach production readiness for some use cases within a few years
Building on a proven design like Linux is much easier than novel OS research, enabling faster progress with a larger talent pool
Such a project could mentor a new generation of Rust-capable kernel hackers while freeing developers from LKML political battles
money_quotes: Every subsystem is a private fiefdom, subject to the whims of each one of Linux's 1,700+ maintainers, almost all of whom have a dog in this race.
Introducing Rust effectively is one part coding work and ninety-nine parts political work – and it's a lot of coding work.
The consequences of these factors is that Rust-for-Linux has become a burnout machine.
Free software is about putting in the work, it's a classical do-ocracy… until it isn't, and people get hurt.
A motivated group of talented Rust OS developers could build a Linux-compatible kernel, from scratch, very quickly, with no need to engage in LKML politics.
I worked on my own novel OS design for a couple of years and it's still stuck in design hell; on the other hand I wrote a passable Unix clone alone in less than 30 days.
Freeing yourselves of the LKML political battles would probably be a big win for the ambitions of bringing Rust into kernel space.
key_insight: Instead of fighting Linux's political culture to integrate Rust, the burned-out Rust-for-Linux developers should channel their talent into building a Linux-compatible kernel from scratch in Rust, where technical progress won't be bottlenecked by maintainer politics.
